relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Human life, born of woman, is brief and trouble-filled.
Like a flower, it appears and withers; like a shadow, it flees.
Job wonders why such fragility is brought into judgment.
He asks who can draw clean from unclean.
Human days are bounded by limits only the Divine knows.
He asks to be looked away from long enough to rest.
A tree still has hope after being cut down.
Even old root and dead stump remain near possibility.
At the scent of water, the tree can bud again.
But when a human falls, Job asks where he is.
Waters can disappear from sea and river.
So humans lie down and do not rise into ordinary waking.
Job longs to be hidden in Sheol until anger passes and remembrance returns.
He wonders whether life can return after death and says he would wait for change.
He imagines a future where God calls and he answers, and the Maker longs for the work of his hands.
Then his steps would be counted without sin being hunted.
His transgression would be sealed away and guilt covered.
Yet mountains crumble and rocks move.
Water wears stone down, and mortal hope erodes.
The human face changes and is sent away.
His children rise or fall beyond his knowing.
Only flesh aches and soul mourns within him.
